prompt: An authentic Japanese Edo-period Ukiyo-e woodblock print on textured washi paper, masterfully executed in the landscape style of Utagawa Hiroshige. The composition utilizes sweeping diagonal leading lines and spatial stacking, deliberately leaving ample negative space in the lower half of the composition to balance the heavy diagonal landmasses. To simulate an authentic physical print, there are slight woodblock registration errors (kento misregistration) visible throughout, with minor, intentional gaps and overlaps between the flat color blocks and the stark black keyblock outlines. The upper sky features a deep indigo high-contrast bokashi (color gradation), with heavy baren suji (woodgrain texture) pressed into the paper. At the highest mountain peak, a Torii gate marks the initialization point. A main path of bright red stepping stones descends. The stones are deeply embedded into the earth's contours, partially obscured by dirt and grass rather than floating on the surface. The stones are spaced far apart at the top, becoming tightly packed. A pilgrim is positioned exactly at the inflection point where the step spacing visibly tightens, illustrating the decaying learning rate. The main path converges at the absolute lowest valley basin, anchored by a serene Shinto shrine beside a pond (the global minimum). A faint secondary path terminates in a higher, separate valley, ending abruptly at a small, weathered, overgrown stone marker (a dosojin), emphasizing its status as a false convergence point (local minimum). Softened contour lines on distant mountains create atmospheric recession via block-wiping. A traditional vertical cartouche contains classical Kanji for '深山幽谷の里'. Authentic hanmoto and kiwame seals sit near the border. Muted indigo, faded ochre, and vermilion palette. Absolutely no AI watermarks, signatures, or modern digital artifacts.
concept: Gradient descent with decaying learning rate, local minima, and global minimum
tradition_approach: Executed in Hiroshige's style with deep indigo bokashi and baren suji texture. Targeted kento misregistration (registration errors) to simulate physical woodblocks. Mapped decaying learning rate to stepping-stone density and global/local minima to specific Shinto architectural markers.
